coredata.dc:title: Luminescent properties of carbon quantum dots synthesized by microplasma method
coredata.dc:creator: Tyutrin, А.А.
Wang, R.
Martynovich, E.F.
coredata.dc:description: The luminescent carbon quantum dots were synthesized by microplasma treatment of a glucose solution at atmospheric pressure at room temperature. The spectral and kinetic characteristics of synthesized quantum dots were studied by time-resolved confocal scanning luminescent microscopy and time-correlated single photon counting. The emission spectra are wide non-elementary bands. Their maxima were located at 476, 500, 547 and 580 nm at the wavelengths of the exciting radiation of 375, 405, 470 and 532 nm, respectively. Three exponential components with decay time constants of 0.65, 2.2, and 6.2 ns were detected in the luminescence kinetics. The presence of these components is due to the formation of three different types of luminescence centers at quantum dots with different probabilities of quantum transitions. Presumably, it may be related to the activation of quantum dots by various impurities or other defects formed luminescence centers. The concentration of quantum dots was controlled by changing the microplasma processing time of the glucose solution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoluminescence
Carbon quantum dots
Microplasma
Decay time constant
Optical properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of the microplasma setup.

Spectra and photographs of the luminescence of the solutions obtained with different plasma processing durations: a) 0 min (untreated solution), b) 30 min, c) 60 min, d) 120 min; luminescence was excited by the radiation of LED with wavelength of 365 nm of the fixed intensity.

Spectra and photographs of the luminescence of the solutions obtained with different plasma processing durations: a) 0 min (untreated solution), b) 30 min, c) 60 min, d) 120 min; luminescence was excited by laser radiation with a wavelength at 405 nm of the fixed intensity.

Luminescence spectra of nanoparticles synthesized by the plasma method (a) and the laser ablation method (b) [15]. Excitation by picosecond laser pulses with a wavelength of 375, 405, 470, 532 nm. We used filters with an L-shaped transmission characteristic with a transmission boundary at 400, 430, 500, 550 nm, respectively.

Kinetics of the growth and decay of the luminescence intensity of quantum dots at different picosecond excitation. The graphs located under the kinetic curves show the degree of correctness of decomposing the luminescence into three time components, the decay constants of which are presented in Table 1. The division into three such components is correct, since the values of these graphs along their entire length are equal to zero.
Luminescence spectra of treated solution excited by laser radiation with a wavelength at 375 nm at optical filters: 1–400 nm, 2–500 nm, 3–550.
Method of band gap energy determination from the Tauc plot.
Normalized particle size distribution obtained from the absorbance spectra at different time processing: 1–30 min, 2–60 min, 3–120 min.
Dependence of the luminescence intensity on plasma processing duration for each the time component at excitation wavelength 375 nm.
Illustration of the tunable fluorescence emission from CQDs containing different functional groups.
The decay time constants (τ) of three luminescence time components and fraction of the intensity (A) for each time component under excitation by lasers with different wavelengths λex.

row.entry:
λexc, nm
Decay time, τ (ns) and fraction, A (%)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Intensities (J) and the decay time constants (τ) of three luminescence time components under excitation by laser with wavelength 375 nm at registering luminescence through cutoff filters.
The fraction of intensities (J) of three luminescence time components under excitation by laser with wavelength 375 nm at registering luminescence through cutoff filters.

head.ce:title: Luminescent properties of carbon quantum dots synthesized by microplasma method

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Irkutsk Branch of Institute of Laser Physics, Siberian Branch, Russian Academy of Science, Irkutsk, 664033, Russia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Irkutsk Branch of Institute of Laser Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Irkutsk Branch of Institute of Laser Physics, Siberian Branch, Russian Academy of Science, Irkutsk, 664033 Russia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100029, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100029, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The luminescent carbon quantum dots were synthesized by microplasma treatment of a glucose solution at atmospheric pressure at room temperature. The spectral and kinetic characteristics of synthesized quantum dots were studied by time-resolved confocal scanning luminescent microscopy and time-correlated single photon counting. The emission spectra are wide non-elementary bands. Their maxima were located at 476, 500, 547 and 580 nm at the wavelengths of the exciting radiation of 375, 405, 470 and 532 nm, respectively. Three exponential components with decay time constants of 0.65, 2.2, and 6.2 ns were detected in the luminescence kinetics. The presence of these components is due to the formation of three different types of luminescence centers at quantum dots with different probabilities of quantum transitions. Presumably, it may be related to the activation of quantum dots by various impurities or other defects formed luminescence centers. The concentration of quantum dots was controlled by changing the microplasma processing time of the glucose solution.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots due to their unique optical, chemical and electrical properties have been extensively studied over the past years. This is primarily caused by their high chemical and physical stability, and no less importantly, their environmental compatibility with biological objects, which cannot be said about metal-semiconductor quantum dots. Indeed, because of their unique properties, carbon quantum dots are used in biomedicine [1], sensors [2–4], optoelectronics [5] and in other branches of science and technology.

Currently, there are several methods for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots, which have both their pros and cons. However, most of them have serious limitations. For example, some of them require the use of toxic reagents (NaOH [6], CHCl3 [7], C2H8N2 [8]), special equipment, high processing temperatures and personnel trained to handle hazardous chemicals. Therefore, researchers are developing more effective and safer methods. One of these methods is the synthesis of quantum dots by the microplasma processing of a solution of carbon-containing substances. The use of this method in comparison with chemical methods has several advantages. The formation of quantum dots occurs in shorter time, the size of the formed dots is more uniform and can be adjusted [9]. For instance, in Ref. [10] R. Wang and colleagues had successfully synthesized gold nanoparticles with use of the microplasma processing method. The gold nanoparticles are used to detect cardiac troponin, a biomarker of heart disease. Wang and colleagues regulated the size of synthesized nanoparticles by adjusting the discharge power. It is also worth noting that the study of the formation of carbon quantum dots by the microplasma processing is currently being intensively conducted [11,12].

Mostly, quantum dots are formed in two ways. Top-down methods when graphite materials serve as a carbon source and bottom-up methods when organic molecules serve as a carbon source. In our work, we combined these two approaches, i.e. we used both the graphite rod and an organic substance - 40% glucose solution - in our experimental setup.

The objective of this work was the formation of carbon quantum dots by the microplasma method, the study of the spectral-kinetic characteristics of their luminescence and identification of the specific features of their synthesis.

The experimental setup is shown in Fig. 1 . A special glass cuvette was filled with the 40% glucose solution (10 ml). At one end of the cuvette, a cylindrical graphite rod 50 mm long and 5 mm in diameter was immersed in the solution. A sharp stainless steel electrode was placed at the other end of the cuvette above the surface of the solution (at distance of 1–2 mm). We used a voltage source previously applied in Refs. [13,14] for the formation of a plasma (positive column) of a glow discharge in a vacuum chamber. Using the adjustable high-voltage source, we applied voltage to the electrodes, in consequence a microplasma was formed on the steel electrode. Microplasma formation occurred at atmospheric pressure in air.

After the plasma processing, the treated samples of the solution were filtered through a 0.22-μm membrane filter to remove the larger particles and impurities. Then they were examined on a PicoQuant GmbH MicroTime 200 time-resolved confocal scanning microscope equipped with an Ocean Optics QE65000 spectrometer.

The luminescence intensity primarily depends on the concentration of quantum dots, therefore we must be able to control it for various scientific and technical applications. One of the parameters for adjusting the concentration of quantum dots is the plasma processing duration. The spectra and photographs of the luminescence of solutions obtained with different plasma treatment durations excited by radiation with wavelengths of 365 nm and 405 nm are shown in Fig. 2 and Fig. 3
. The voltage and current values in all cases were fixed and amounted to U = 3.3 kV, I = 5 mA.
The positions of the maxima of the luminescence bands are about 480 nm at excitation 365 nm and 500 nm at excitation 405 nm. The peak at 463 nm in Fig. 5 is caused by Raman scattering by water.

Four spectra of the carbon quantum dots solution luminescence excited by picosecond laser pulses with a wavelength of 375, 405, 470, 532 nm are shown in Fig. 4
. The solution undergone plasma processing for duration of 2 h at a voltage of 3.3 kV and a current of 5 mA. The form of the luminescence spectra indicates that the maxima of the luminescence spectra of the particles formed during processing shift with a change in the wavelength of the exciting radiation. The luminescence non-elementary bands with maxima at 476, 500, 547, 580 nm were observed under excitation by lasers with wavelengths of 375, 405, 470, 532 nm respectively. Comparing these emission bands with the luminescence bands of natural diamond nanoparticles obtained by laser ablation [15], we found a great similarity between them. In both cases, the shift of the spectral bands and the positions of their maxima almost coincided. Hence, we conclude that the particles we had synthesized are the diamond-like carbon quantum dots. Also, the obtained data are in good agreement with other works [16,17], in which only glucose without additional reagents is used as the initial precursor. The presence of additional reagents at synthesis can significantly change the luminescent properties of QD's.

The luminescence kinetics of quantum dots was also measured with use of the confocal microscope MicroTime 200 (Fig. 5). Using programmed decomposition (SymPhoTime, TimeHarp 200), we found that the luminescence of quantum dots under excitation of lasers with wavelengths of 375, 405, 470, 532 nm has three exponential time components. The decay time constants of these components are shown in Table 1 . Their values are equal to 0.65, 2.2, and 6.2 ns, with measurement errors ∼20%. The relatively high value of the error in these measurements is due to the complexity of the luminescence kinetics and the need to decompose the luminescence into components.

It is currently known the photoluminescence of quantum dots can be explained by:
•
interband luminescence (luminescence of carbon core states);
•
defect states of carbon quantum dots that form luminescence centers;
•
surface centers of luminescence (surface oxidation, surface functional groups).

If we generalize these mechanisms of luminescence of quantum dots to two models, then we can distinguish the luminescence of quantum dots, which depends on the particle size (quantum size effect) and which does not depend on the particle size (defect and surface luminescence centers).

The shift of the maximum of the luminescence spectrum at changing the wavelength of the exciting radiation in Fig. 4 for quantum dots could be explained by one of two models. First, it may be due to the quantum size effect. A similar effect was observed, for example, in the work of Donega and Koole [18]. In this work, it was shown theoretically and experimentally that with an increase in the size of a quantum dot, the band gap decreases, which leads to a decrease in the energy of the emitted luminescence quanta. Therefore, the luminescence spectrum shifts to longer wavelengths with increasing particle size. Therefore, within the framework of the first model, it is necessary to assume that the short-wavelength part of the emission spectrum is due to small particles, and the long-wavelength part is due to particles of a larger size. In order to test this interpretation, we conducted the following experiment. First of all, we took into account that the particles with a short-wave spectrum according to Ref. [18] should have luminescence of short duration, and larger particles, respectively, have a longer duration. Therefore, the ratio of the intensities of time components with different durations was studied at spectral shift of the luminescence registration region using cutoff filters. The results are shown in Fig. 6 and in Tables 2 and 3
. As follows from the data in Table 3, the ratios of the intensities of the three temporal components do not change at spectral shift of the luminescence registration region within the error range (∼20%). It is contradicting to the explanation of the presented experimental facts based on model [18]. It means that the origin of the three fixed time components is weakly related to the size effect.

Several methods are known for determining the size of quantum dots. The main methods include transmission electron microscopy (TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS), X-ray scattering (SAXS), alytical ultracentrifugation (AUC), field-flow fractionation. Each of these methods have its own positive and negative characteristics. In our work, we used alternative methods to determine the size of the synthesized particles.

In order to determine the band gap synthesized by CQD's we used the Tauc's relation:
(1)
α h v = C
( h v −
E g
b u l k
)
n
,
where α is the absorption coefficient, hν is the photon energy, Eg bulk is the material band gap, n has a value of 1/2 for direct-gap and 2 for indirect-gap transitions. It is known amorphous carbon and graphite have a zero band gap. However, in Refs. [19–21], the value of the band gap of the material for CQD's was estimated and tested, which is ∼1.9 eV. Also, in our work for CQD's, the value n = 1\2 was used, because this value has good agreement with experiment [19,22–24].

The optical absorption spectra in Tauc plot is presented in Fig. 7 . It was obtained by a PerkinElmer Lambda 950 UV/VIS/NIR spectrophotometer. Using the absorption spectrum of the synthesized CQD's, and the Taus equation, we obtained the band gap of the CQD's, which is ∼5.5 eV (Fig. 7).

The average size of the synthesized particles can be estimated from the Bruce equation based on the effective mass model [25]:
(2)
E ∗
≅
E g
b u l k
+
ħ 2
π 2
2 e
r 2
(
1
m e
m o
+
1
m e
m o
)
−
1.8 e
4 π ε
ε 0
r
where Е* - band gap, Eg bulk – bulk band gap, r – particle radius, me and mh – effective mass of the electrons and the holes, ε – the relative permittivity, ε 0 – permittivity of free space.

Using the average CQD's band gap obtained from the Taus equation and equation (2), we calculated the average CQD's size assuming Eg bulk = 1.9 eV, me = mh = 0.035 m [19]. The average size (diameter) of the synthesized CQD's is ∼4.7 nm.
Comparison with experimental results showed Bruce's model gives more correct results than other models (Hyperbolic Band Model (HBM), Henglein's formula, Yu's formula) [26].

Another alternative method for determining the size of synthesized particles is the analysis of inhomogeneous broadening observed in the optical absorption spectrum of nanoparticles. The method was proposed by Pesika et al. [27,28] and tested by other researchers [29,30]. Pesika et al. state the particle size distribution can be obtained by assuming the absorption A at any wavelength refers to the entire volume of particles with a radius greater than or equal to the size corresponding to the absorption edge. Then the particle size distribution will be described by the following equation [27]:
(3)
n
( r )
≅
−
d A / d r
4 3
π
r 3

Fig. 8 shows the normalized particle size distribution obtained using equations (2) and (3) from the absorption spectra of synthesized CQD's for samples with different microplasma treatment times (30, 60, 120 min, respectively).

Based on Fig. 8, we can conclude after microplasma treatment with a 40% glucose solution, CQD's with a size of ∼4.4 nm (4.2–4.6 nm) were predominantly formed. The figure also shows a wide distribution of particles with a size of 5–6.5 nm, but with a lower concentration. It should also be noted the concentration of CQD's increases with increasing microplasma treatment time. However, the largest increase in CQD's is observed for particles ∼4.4 nm in size.

From Fig. 8, it can be assumed the slow component τ3 of luminescence refers to particles with sizes of ∼5–6.5 nm, but if we consider the dependence of the luminescence intensity of the three time components on the time of microplasma treatment (Fig. 9 ), we will see the luminescence intensity I3 for slow component τ3 constantly grows with increasing time of microplasma treatment. However, from the distribution of particles by size (Fig. 8), the concentration of particles with a size of ∼5–6.5 nm is practically the same (curves 2 and 3 of Fig. 8). Therefore, we can conclude that the slow component τ3 refers to particles with sizes of ∼4.2–4.6 nm.

We state the synthesized CQD's are predominantly 4.4 ± 0.2 nm in size and have multi emission behavior according to the model shown in Fig. 10 . Upon excitation of CQD's with a fixed radiation wavelength, luminescence occurs in a wide range of radiation due to three quantum transitions with three luminescence decay time constants τ1, τ2, τ3, respectively. We think the presence of three fixed time components of luminescence is due to the formation of three different types of luminescence centers in quantum dots with different probabilities of quantum transitions. Presumably, this may be due to the activation of quantum dots by defects that form luminescence centers.

This model can be confirmed by the experiment with cut-off filters carried out above (Fig. 6), in which it is shown that the intensity ratios of the three temporal components (Table 3) do not change when the spectral region of luminescence is shifted. In the case when we have particles, for example, with 3 different sizes, then the intensity ratios would differ significantly from each other

It is difficult to explain the obtained results of synthesized quantum dots only by the quantum confinement effect. Due to the fact that we used the plasma method at atmospheric pressure to synthesize QDs, during irradiation, the plasma creates various ions (O2 +, N2 +), radicals (NO, NO2) and molecules that can interact with the solution, which can lead to the formation of quantum dots with surface states. Therefore, the results obtained should be considered as a synergistic effect of the quantum confinement effect and the presence of surface states, for example, oxygen-functional groups (CO, C–O–C, –OH, COOH). It could be assumed the fast luminescence component (τ1 = 0.65 ns) corresponds to the π – π* (С-С) interband transition of the carbon core. However, in this case, with an increase in the excitation wavelength, the intensity value for this component should decrease drastically, which is not observed in our case (Table 1). This suggests a different nature of the transition. Slow luminescence decay components (τ2 = 2.2, and τ3 = 6.2 ns) are traditionally associated with n – π* transitions of oxygen functional groups. The presence of certain functional groups on CD's is largely determined by the original precursor that is used in the synthesis. For instance, in the article [31], folic acid (FA) is used as a precursor in the synthesis of CD's. Comparison of the FTIR spectra synthesized by CD's based on this precursor and the precursor itself (folic acid) shows that CD's have similar functional groups (CO, O–H) that are present in the precursor. In our case, glucose is used as a precursor, which has intense CO, C–O–C, –OH bands, which determines the presence of such functional groups in CD's when synthesized in glucose.

It should be noted the absence of a pronounced quantum-size effect in the synthesized nanoparticles can be explained by the fact that we have formed carbon nanodots (CNDs), in accordance with the classification presented in Ref. [31]. In such particles, the quantum size effect is not observed, and the luminescence mechanism is realized due to superposition of responses of assembled individual emitters (e.g. fluorophore molecules or emitter functional groups generally located on the nanoparticle surface) [32].

Quantum dots formed by the microplasma processing of glucose solution have a wide spectra of luminescence. The luminescence contains three time components with the decay time constants of 0.65; 2.2 and 6.2 ns at the measurement error about ∼20%. The presence of these components is due to the formation of three different types of luminescence centers at quantum dots with different probabilities of quantum transitions. Presumably, it may be related to the activation of quantum dots by various surface states, defects formed luminescence centers. The concentration of quantum dots is smoothly controlled by the magnitude of the microplasma treatment time. The method can be successfully used for synthesis of luminescent carbon quantum dots for scientific and technical applications.

Alexander A. Tyutrin: Methodology, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Ruixue Wang: Conceptualization, Supervision. Evgeny F. Martynovich: Methodology, Conceptualization, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research was supported by the Ministry of Science and Higher Education of the Russian Federation, the Basic Research Plan of the Russian Academy of Sciences, Project No. 0243-2021-0004, the State Assignment, Project No. FZZE-2020-0017.
